sources.notes: Count arrays c of length n over [1,m] that contain every beautiful choice array as a subsequence.
Official reference code builds one polynomial for each possible required position depth up to 5 and multiplies them with NTT.

annotation.summary: Count length-n arrays over [1,m] that contain every array formed by choosing one element from each given small set as a subsequence.
annotation.constraints: n is up to 2e5, m is huge, each required set has size at most 5, and the total required length is at most n.
annotation.core_idea: The mandatory part of any perfect array must be able to realize all choices from the k sets in order. Since each set size is at most 5, group positions by depth i: how many sets contain an i-th choice. For each depth, the extra filler elements before that depth have a generating function whose coefficient counts ways to distribute unused elements with colors not reserved by earlier choices. The final count is the product of these small-depth generating functions, multiplied by factorials for the internal order of choices in each set; polynomial multiplications are done with NTT under 998244353.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) from multiplying truncated polynomials.

solution_variants.name: generating-functions-ntt
solution_variants.summary: Build one polynomial per depth 0..5, multiply them with NTT, and take the coefficient for the remaining free positions.

tags.evidence: The solution represents choices for extra filler positions as generating functions and extracts one coefficient.
The official implementation uses NTT-style convolution to multiply the depth polynomials efficiently.
The count uses stars-and-bars/binomial coefficients and factorial factors for the required subsequence choices.
The polynomial product is equivalent to DP over used filler length across depth groups.
